title: Lamb Ouzi Whole Slow Roasted

seo.title: Authentic Lamb Ouzi Recipe: Whole Slow Roasted Perfection
seo.metaDescription: Master authentic Middle Eastern Lamb Ouzi with our whole slow roasted recipe. Achieve incredibly tender lamb, perfect for a feast. Get the full recipe here!
lowCompetitionKeywords: whole slow roasted lamb ouzi dinner
seo.h1: Lamb Ouzi: Whole Slow Roasted Middle Eastern Lamb Recipe
h2Headings: Ingredients for Whole Slow Roasted Lamb Ouzi
Tips for Perfect Slow Roasted Lamb
seo.imageAlt: A beautifully presented whole slow roasted Lamb Ouzi dish with rice and nuts, ready to serve.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the rich flavors of the Middle East with this show-stopping whole slow roasted Lamb Ouzi, perfect for your next special occasion.

description: Lamb Ouzi is a magnificent Middle Eastern dish featuring tender, slow roasted lamb served over aromatic spiced rice, often garnished with nuts and fresh herbs. This recipe guides you through creating a show-stopping centerpiece perfect for special occasions or a grand family feast. The rich flavors and melt-in-your-mouth texture make it an unforgettable culinary experience.

instructions.title: Prepare and Marinate the Lamb
instructions.text: Pat the lamb leg or shoulder dry. Score the fat cap with a sharp knife in a diamond pattern. In a small bowl, mix 1 tablespoon of baharat, 1 teaspoon salt, 1/2 teaspoon cinnamon, 1/4 teaspoon cardamom, and 1 tablespoon olive oil. Rub this spice mixture all over the lamb, ensuring it gets into the scored areas. Cover and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes, or preferably overnight for deeper flavor.

instructions.title: Sear the Lamb
instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 160°C (325°F). In a large, oven-safe pot or Dutch oven, heat 2 tablespoons of olive oil or ghee over medium-high heat. Sear the lamb on all sides until deeply browned, about 5-7 minutes per side. This develops rich flavor and color.

instructions.title: Slow Roast the Lamb
instructions.text: Add 2 cups of hot water or lamb broth to the pot with the seared lamb. Bring to a simmer, then cover the pot tightly and transfer it to the preheated oven. Roast for 3 to 3.5 hours, or until the lamb is fork-tender and easily pulls away from the bone. Baste occasionally with the pan juices. If the liquid evaporates, add more hot water or broth as needed. Once tender, remove the lamb from the pot, tent with foil, and let it rest while you prepare the rice. Strain the pan juices and reserve them.

instructions.title: Prepare the Spiced Rice Base
instructions.text: While the lamb rests, in a large saucepan, melt 1 tablespoon of ghee or olive oil over medium heat. Add the chopped onion and sauté until softened and translucent, about 5-7 minutes. Add the minced garlic, remaining 1 tablespoon baharat, 1/2 teaspoon cinnamon, 1/4 teaspoon cardamom, whole cloves, and black peppercorns. Cook for 1-2 minutes until fragrant, stirring constantly.

instructions.title: Cook the Ouzi Rice
instructions.text: Drain the soaked basmati rice and add it to the saucepan with the sautéed aromatics and spices. Stir gently to coat the rice evenly. Pour in 4 cups of hot water or lamb broth, and add 1 teaspoon of salt. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil, then reduce the heat to low, cover the saucepan tightly, and simmer for 15-20 minutes, or until all the liquid has been absorbed and the rice is tender. Do not lift the lid during cooking.

instructions.title: Assemble and Serve
instructions.text: Once the rice is cooked, remove it from the heat and let it rest, covered, for 5 minutes. Fluff the rice gently with a fork. Transfer the spiced rice to a large serving platter. Carefully place the slow roasted lamb on top of the rice. Pour some of the reserved pan juices over the lamb and rice. Garnish generously with toasted pine nuts, blanched almonds, and fresh chopped parsley. Serve immediately with a side of yogurt or a simple salad.

tips: For truly authentic flavor, consider using homemade Baharat spice blend. Toasting and grinding your own spices just before use will elevate the aroma and taste significantly.
If a whole lamb leg or shoulder is too large, you can adapt this recipe using lamb shanks or a boneless lamb roast, adjusting the cooking time accordingly until fork-tender.

Leftover Lamb Ouzi can be stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days. Reheat gently, adding a splash of broth to the rice if it seems dry, and serve with fresh yogurt or a cucumber salad.
article:
Prepare to embark on a culinary journey to the heart of the Middle East, where flavors are rich, aromas are intoxicating, and hospitality reigns supreme. Few dishes embody this spirit as magnificently as Lamb Ouzi, a show-stopping centerpiece perfect for special occasions and family gatherings. Imagine succulent lamb, slow-roasted to fall-off-the-bone perfection, nestled amidst a bed of aromatic, spiced rice, studded with nuts and often peas. This isn't just a meal; it's an experience, a celebration of heritage and taste that will leave a lasting impression on your guests.

While often prepared for large feasts, mastering the art of whole slow roasted Lamb Ouzi at home is a rewarding endeavor. This guide will walk you through everything you need to know to create this incredible Middle Eastern dinner recipe, turning your kitchen into a hub of exotic aromas and delicious anticipation.

## What is Lamb Ouzi?

Lamb Ouzi (sometimes spelled Ouze or Oozie) is a traditional Middle Eastern dish, particularly popular in the Levant and Gulf regions. At its core, it’s a celebratory dish, renowned for its tender, slow-cooked lamb and flavorful spiced rice. While there are variations across different countries and even within families, the essence remains the same: a grand presentation of perfectly cooked lamb, often a whole leg or shoulder, served on a mound of fragrant rice.

Historically, Ouzi was prepared for significant events like weddings, Eid al-Adha, and other large family gatherings, symbolizing abundance and generosity. The slow roasting process ensures the lamb is incredibly tender and infused with a symphony of spices, making it a truly unforgettable culinary experience. The rice, cooked with broth and a special blend of seasonings, complements the richness of the meat beautifully, creating a harmonious and utterly satisfying meal.

## Ingredients for Whole Slow Roasted Lamb Ouzi

To create an authentic and mouth-watering Lamb Ouzi, sourcing quality ingredients is key. Here’s what you’ll need:

For the Lamb:
*   1 whole bone-in lamb leg or shoulder (5-7 lbs/2.2-3.2 kg)
*   3-4 cloves garlic, minced
*   1/4 cup olive oil
*   2 tablespoons lemon juice
*   2 tablespoons Baharat spice blend (or a mix of 1 tsp each: cumin, coriander, paprika, turmeric; 1/2 tsp each: cinnamon, black pepper, cardamom, cloves)
*   1 tablespoon salt
*   1 teaspoon black pepper
*   Optional: A few sprigs of fresh rosemary or thyme

For the Ouzi Rice:
*   3 cups Basmati rice, rinsed until water runs clear
*   1 large onion, finely chopped
*   2 cloves garlic, minced
*   2 tablespoons olive oil or ghee
*   4 cups lamb or chicken broth
*   1 cup frozen peas (thawed)
*   1/2 cup toasted slivered almonds or pine nuts (for garnish and mixing)
*   1 tablespoon Baharat spice blend (or a mix of 1 tsp each: cumin, coriander, paprika; 1/2 tsp each: turmeric, cinnamon, black pepper)
*   1 teaspoon salt
*   1/2 teaspoon black pepper

For Garnish and Serving:
*   Fresh parsley or mint, chopped
*   Extra toasted nuts
*   Lemon wedges
*   Optional: Plain yogurt or a simple tahini sauce

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Lamb Ouzi

Preparing this impressive dish is a labor of love, but the steps are straightforward and the results are incredibly rewarding.

1.  Prepare the Lamb:
    *   Pat the lamb leg dry with paper towels. Using a sharp knife, score the surface of the lamb in a diamond pattern, about 1/2 inch deep. This helps the marinade penetrate and allows fat to render.
    *   In a small bowl, combine the minced garlic, olive oil, lemon juice, Baharat spice blend, salt, and black pepper. Mix well to form a paste.
    *   Generously rub the spice paste all over the lamb, ensuring it gets into the scored cuts. If using, tuck rosemary or thyme sprigs into the cuts.
    *   Place the lamb in a large roasting pan, cover, and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight, to allow the flavors to meld.

2.  Slow Roast the Lamb:
    *   Preheat your oven to 325°F (160°C).
    *   Remove the lamb from the refrigerator about an hour before roasting to bring it closer to room temperature.
    *   Cover the roasting pan tightly with foil. This creates a steamy environment that tenderizes the meat.
    *   Roast for 3-4 hours, or until an internal temperature of 145°F (63°C) for medium-rare or 160°F (71°C) for medium is reached. For fall-off-the-bone tenderness, continue roasting until it reaches around 190-200°F (88-93°C), which might take 5-6 hours or even more depending on the size of your lamb.
    *   During the last hour of cooking, remove the foil to allow the lamb to brown and crisp up on the outside. Baste occasionally with the pan juices.
    *   Once cooked, remove the lamb from the oven, transfer it to a carving board, and tent it loosely with foil. Let it rest for at least 20-30 minutes before carving. This is crucial for juicy, tender meat.

3.  Prepare the Ouzi Rice:
    *   While the lamb is resting, prepare the rice. In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat olive oil or ghee over medium heat. Add the chopped onion and sauté until softened and translucent, about 5-7 minutes. Add the minced garlic and cook for another minute until fragrant.
    *   Stir in the rinsed Basmati rice, Baharat spice blend, salt, and black pepper. Toast the rice and spices for 2-3 minutes, stirring constantly, to enhance their flavor.
    *   Pour in the lamb or chicken broth. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover tightly, and simmer for 15-18 minutes, or until all the liquid is absorbed and the rice is tender. Do not lift the lid during cooking.
    *   Remove from heat and let sit, covered, for another 10 minutes.
    *   Gently fluff the rice with a fork. Stir in the thawed frozen peas and half of the toasted nuts.

4.  Assemble and Serve:
    *   On a large serving platter, create a generous bed of the Ouzi rice.
    *   Carefully place the whole slow roasted Lamb Ouzi in the center of the rice.
    *   Garnish generously with fresh chopped parsley or mint and the remaining toasted nuts. Serve with lemon wedges on the side.

## Tips for Perfect Slow Roasted Lamb

Achieving the perfect, melt-in-your-mouth lamb is easier than you think with these expert tips:

*   Quality Matters: Start with a good quality lamb leg or shoulder. Bone-in cuts tend to offer more flavor and moisture.
*   Marinate for Flavor: Don't skimp on marinating time. The longer the lamb sits in the spice rub, the deeper the flavors will penetrate, resulting in a more aromatic and tasty Lamb Ouzi.
*   Low and Slow is the Way to Go: Resist the urge to crank up the heat. A low oven temperature over an extended period breaks down tough fibers, ensuring unparalleled tenderness.
*   Resting is Essential: After roasting, always allow the lamb to rest. This allows the juices to redistribute throughout the meat, resulting in a more succulent and flavorful cut. Skipping this step can lead to dry lamb.
*   Use a Meat Thermometer: For precise cooking, a reliable meat thermometer is your best friend. Insert it into the thickest part of the lamb, avoiding the bone.
*   Baste for Moisture: While covered, the lamb will self-baste. Once uncovered, baste it every 30-45 minutes with the pan juices to keep it moist and help develop a beautiful golden crust.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Lamb Ouzi is a complete meal in itself, but it truly shines when accompanied by a few fresh sides that balance its richness.

*   Fresh Salads: A vibrant Fattoush salad (with crisp vegetables, toasted pita, and a tangy sumac dressing) or a simple Tabbouleh (parsley and bulgur salad) provides a refreshing counterpoint.
*   Yogurt or Tahini Sauce: A dollop of plain yogurt, perhaps mixed with a little garlic and mint, or a simple tahini-lemon sauce, can add a creamy tang that complements the lamb beautifully.
*   Pickles and Olives: A platter of mixed Middle Eastern pickles and briny olives can cleanse the palate and add another layer of flavor.
*   Fresh Bread: Warm pita bread or Arabic flatbread is perfect for scooping up any remaining rice and juices.
*   Beverages: For drinks, consider a refreshing glass of Ayran (a savory yogurt drink), fresh mint tea, or sparkling water with lemon.

## Variations and Substitutions

While the classic Lamb Ouzi is magnificent, don't hesitate to experiment with variations to suit your taste or available ingredients.

*   Lamb Cut: If a whole leg is too large, you can use a bone-in lamb shoulder or even individual lamb shanks. Adjust cooking times accordingly.
*   Spice Blends: If you can't find Baharat, you can create your own blend using the suggested individual spices (cumin, coriander, paprika, turmeric, cinnamon, black pepper, cardamom, cloves) or experiment with other Middle Eastern spice mixes.
*   Rice Fillings: Beyond peas and nuts, consider adding cooked chickpeas, dried cranberries or apricots for a touch of sweetness, or even small pieces of sautéed ground lamb or beef to the rice for extra richness.
*   Garnish: While parsley and nuts are traditional, fresh mint or pomegranate seeds can add a beautiful pop of color and freshness.
*   Cooking Method for Lamb: While slow roasting is highly recommended for a whole lamb cut, smaller pieces of lamb can be braised in a pot on the stovetop or even in an Instant Pot for a quicker, though slightly different, result.

Bringing the tradition of Lamb Ouzi to your table is more than just cooking; it’s an act of love and celebration. This majestic Middle Eastern dinner recipe, with its tender lamb and aromatic rice, promises to be a memorable feast that will transport your senses and delight your guests. So, gather your ingredients, embrace the slow cooking process, and prepare to create a culinary masterpiece that truly embodies the warmth and richness of Middle Eastern hospitality.
